questions.prompt: If today is Wednesday, what day is 14 days from today?
hints: Adding 0 days keeps the day-of-week the same.
questions.explanation: 14 days = 2 weeks. No shift. Still Wednesday.

questions.prompt: If today is Monday, what day is 100 days from today?

questions.promptTex: \text{If } d = 4 \text{ (Thursday) and we ask 'what day is it 60 days from now?', the answer day is } x. \\ \text{Find } x \text{ (0 = Sunday ... 6 = Saturday).}

questions.prompt: How many days does a non-leap year shift the day of the week?
questions.explanation: $365 = 52 \cdot 7 + 1$, so $365 \bmod 7 = 1$. Non-leap years shift the day-of-week by 1.

questions.promptTex: \text{If Jan 1 was a Sunday in a non-leap year, what day is Jan 1 of next year?}

questions.prompt: Today is Tuesday. The 1st of last month was 31 days ago. What day of the week was it?

questions.explanation: $4 \cdot 1 + 1 = 5$ (using $365 \bmod 7 = 1$ and one extra day for the leap year). Verify: $1461 / 7 = 208.71...$, $1461 - 1456 = 5$. ✓

steps.text: **Recognize the structure.** $365 \cdot 4 + 1$ counts days over 4 years (with 1 leap year adding the extra day). We want this mod 7.

**Smarter approach: use modular arithmetic step by step instead of computing 1461 first.** Modular arithmetic respects multiplication and addition: $(a \cdot b) \bmod n = ((a \bmod n) \cdot (b \bmod n)) \bmod n$.
**Apply.** $365 \bmod 7 = 1$ (since $365 = 52 \cdot 7 + 1$). So $(365 \cdot 4) \bmod 7 \equiv (1 \cdot 4) = 4$. Add the leap-year extra day: $4 + 1 = 5$.
**Lesson.** When numbers are large, don't compute the full product first — reduce mod $n$ at every step. Saves big arithmetic.

questions.prompt: Why does day-of-week arithmetic only care about the date modulo 7?
choices: Because the calendar repeats exactly every 7 days.
hints: After 7 days the day-of-week loops back.
questions.explanation: The week is a 7-day cycle. Adding 7 days returns you to the same day-of-week. So only the remainder mod 7 matters for day-of-week shifts.
